element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Tutorial-implicit; players expected to understand shmup basics from series conventions
Button combination inputs (Street Fighter-style) per Ai Cho Aniki sequel design philosophy
Increases in complexity as series progresses through sequels
Pattern memorization required; escalates across game progression

rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Fast-paced arcade-style shmup with escalating difficulty; maintains consistent intensity across stages with rhythmic boss punctuation

difficulty_curve.early_game: Introductory shmup difficulty; establishes control responsiveness and basic enemy patterns
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Escalating enemy density and pattern complexity; power-up management becomes strategic
difficulty_curve.late_game: Dense bullet patterns and demanding boss encounters requiring memorization and precision
notable_spikes: Difficulty scaling between original Cho Aniki and Ai Cho Aniki (sequel introduces Street Fighter-style combo requirements)

notable_level_patterns.description: Side-scrolling progression with left-right movement freedom; enemies approach from right screen edge; boss encounters terminate stages
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Generic stage structure applies across series

notable_level_patterns.description: Series plays in vein of Gradius with power-up mechanics and progressive difficulty; established shmup formula followed

sources_used: wikipedia - Cho Aniki series overview and game-specific mechanics descriptions

data_notes.limitation: TCRF source contained access restrictions and was not usable. Wikipedia source provides series context and Ai Cho Aniki sequel mechanics but lacks granular level-by-level design documentation for original SNES entry.
